html:
IV

Vincent and the Comte de Passavant

Mon p√®re √©tait une b√™te, mais ma m√®re avait de lвАЩesprit; elle √©tait qui√©tiste; cвАЩ√©tait une petite femme douce qui me disait souvent: Mon fils, vous serez damn√©. Mais cela ne lui faisait pas de peine.

Fontenelle

No, it was not to see his mistress that Vincent Molinier went out every evening. Quickly as he walks, let us follow him. He goes along the Rue Notre Dame des Champs, at the further end of which he lives, until he reaches the Rue Placide, which is its prolongation; then he turns down the Rue du Bac, where there are still a few belated passersby. In the Rue de Babylone, he stops in front of a porte-coch√®re which swings open to let him in. The Comte de Passavant lives here. If Vincent were not in the habit of coming often, he would enter this sumptuous mansion with a less confident air. The footman who comes to the door knows well enough how much timidity this feigned assurance hides. Vincent, with a touch of affectation, instead of handing him his hat, tosses it on to an armchair.

It is only recently that Vincent has taken to coming here. Robert de Passavant, who now calls himself his friend, is the friend of a great many people. I am not very sure how he and Vincent became acquainted. At the lyc√©e, I expectвБ†вАФthough Robert de Passavant is perceptibly older than Vincent; they had lost sight of each other for several years and then, quite lately, had met again one evening when, by some unusual chance, Olivier had gone with his brother to the theatre; during the entrвАЩacte Passavant had invited them both to take an ice with him; he had learnt that Vincent had just finished his last medical examinations and was undecided as to whether he should take a place as house physician in a hospital; science attracted him more than medicine, but the necessity of earning his livingвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ in short, Vincent accepted with pleasure the very remunerative offer Robert de Passavant had made him a little later of coming every evening, to attend his old father, who had lately undergone a very serious operation; it was a matter of bandages, of injections, of soundingsвБ†вАФin fact, of whatever delicate services you please, which necessitate the ministrations of an expert hand.

But, added to this, the Vicomte had secret reasons for wishing a nearer acquaintance with Vincent; and Vincent had still others for consenting. RobertвАЩs secret reason we shall try to discover later on. As for VincentвАЩsвБ†вАФit was this: he was urgently in need of money. When your heart is in the right place and a wholesome education has early instilled into you a sense of your responsibilities, you donвАЩt get a woman with child, without feeling yourself more or less bound to herвБ†вАФespecially when the woman has left her husband to follow you.

Up till then, Vincent had lived on the whole virtuously. His adventure with Laura appeared to him alternately, according to the moment of the day in which he thought of it, as either monstrous or perfectly natural. It very often suffices to add together a quantity of little facts which, taken separately, are very simple and very natural, to arrive at a sum which is monstrous. He said all this to himself over and over again as he walked along, but it didnвАЩt get him out of his difficulties. No doubt, he had never thought of taking this woman permanently under his protectionвБ†вАФof marrying her after a divorce, or of living with her without marrying; he was obliged to confess to himself that he had no very violent passion for her; but he knew she was in Paris without means of subsistence; he was the cause of her distress; at the very least he owed her that first precarious aid which he felt himself less and less able to give herвБ†вАФless today than yesterday. For last week he still possessed the five thousand francs which his mother had patiently and laboriously saved to give him a start in his profession; those five thousand francs would have sufficed, no doubt, to pay for his mistressвАЩs confinement, for her stay in a nursing home, for the childвАЩs first necessaries. To what demonвАЩs advice then had he listened? What demon had hinted to him one evening that this sum which he had as good as given to Laura, which he had laid by for her, pledged to herвБ†вАФthat this sum would be insufficient? No, it was not Robert de Passavant; Robert had never said anything of the kind; but his proposal to take Vincent with him to a gambling club fell out precisely the same evening. And Vincent had accepted.

The hell in question was a particularly treacherous one, inasmuch as the habitu√©s were all people in society and the whole thing took place on a friendly footing. Robert introduced his friend Vincent to one and another. Vincent, who was taken unawares, was not able to play high that first evening. He had hardly anything on him and refused the notes which the Vicomte offered to advance him. But as he began by winning, he regretted not being able to stake more and promised to go back the next night.

вАЬEverybody knows you now; thereвАЩs no need for me to come with you again,вАЭ said Robert.

These meetings took place at Pierre de BrouvilleвАЩs, commonly known as Pedro. After this first evening Robert de Passavant had put his car at his friendвАЩs disposal. Vincent used to look in about eleven oвАЩclock, smoke a cigarette with Robert, and after chatting for ten minutes or so, go upstairs. His stay there was more or less lengthy according to the CountвАЩs patience, temper or requirements; after this he drove in the car to PedroвАЩs in the Rue St.¬†Florentin, whence about an hour later the car took him backвБ†вАФnot actually to his own door, for he was afraid of attracting attention, but to the nearest corner.

The night before last, Laura Douviers, seated on the steps which led to the MoliniersвАЩ flat, had waited for Vincent till three oвАЩclock in the morning; it was not till then that he had come in. As a matter of fact, Vincent had not been at PedroвАЩs that night. Two days had gone by since he had lost every penny of the five thousand francs. He had informed Laura of this; he had written that he could do nothing more for her; that he advised her to go back to her husband or her fatherвБ†вАФto confess everything. But things had gone so far, that confession seemed impossible to Laura and she could not contemplate it with any sort of calm. Her loverвАЩs objurgations merely aroused indignation in herвБ†вАФan indignation which only subsided to leave her a prey to despair. This was the state in which Vincent had found her. She had tried to keep him; he had torn himself from her grasp. Doubtless, he had to steel himself to do it, for he had a tender heart; but he was more of a pleasure-seeker than a lover and he had easily persuaded himself that duty itself demanded harshness. He had answered nothing to all her entreaties and lamentations, and as Olivier, who had heard them, told Bernard afterwards, when Vincent shut the door against her, she had sunk down on the steps and remained for a long time sobbing in the dark.

More than forty hours had gone by since that night. The day before, Vincent had not gone to Robert de PassavantвАЩs, whose father seemed to be recovering; but that evening a telegram had summoned him. Robert wished to see him. When Vincent entered the room in which Robert usually satвБ†вАФa room which he used as his study and smoking-room and which he had been at some pains to decorate and fit up in his own fashionвБ†вАФRobert carelessly held out his hand to him over his shoulder, without rising.

Robert is writing. He is sitting at a bureau littered with books. Facing him the French window which gives on to the garden, stands wide open in the moonlight. He speaks without turning round.

вАЬDo you know what I am writing? But you wonвАЩt mention it, will you? You promise, eh?вБ†вАФa manifesto for the opening number of DhurmerвАЩs review. I shanвАЩt sign it, of courseвБ†вАФespecially as I puff myself in it.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ And then as itвАЩll certainly come out in the long run that IвАЩm financing it, I donвАЩt want it known too soon that I write for it. So mumвАЩs the word! But itвАЩs just occurred to meвБ†вАФdidnвАЩt you say that young brother of yours wrote? WhatвАЩs his name again?вАЭ

вАЬOlivier,вАЭ says Vincent.

вАЬOlivier! Yes; I had forgotten. DonвАЩt stay standing there like that! Sit down in that armchair. YouвАЩre not cold? Shall I shut the window?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ ItвАЩs poetry he writes, isnвАЩt it? He ought to bring me something to see. Of course, I donвАЩt promise to take it.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ But, all the same, I should be surprised if it were bad. He looks an intelligent boy. And then heвАЩs obviously au courant. I should like to talk to him. Tell him to come and see me, eh? Mind, I count on it. A cigarette?вАЭ And he holds out his silver cigarette-case.

вАЬWith pleasure.вАЭ

вАЬNow then, Vincent, listen to me. I must speak to you very seriously. You behaved like a child the other eveningвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ so did I, for that matter. I donвАЩt say it was wrong of me to take you to PedroвАЩs, but I feel responsible, a little, for the money youвАЩve lost. I donвАЩt know if thatвАЩs whatвАЩs meant by remorse, but, upon my word, itвАЩs beginning to disturb my sleep and my digestion. And then, when I think of that unhappy woman you told me about.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ But thatвАЩs another story. We wonвАЩt speak of that. ItвАЩs sacred. What I want to say is thisвБ†вАФthat I wishвБ†вАФyes, IвАЩm absolutely determined to put at your disposal a sum of money equivalent to what youвАЩve lost. It was five thousand francs, wasnвАЩt it? And youвАЩre to risk it again. Once more, I repeat, I consider myself the cause of your losing this moneyвБ†вАФI owe it to youвБ†вАФthereвАЩs no need to thank me. YouвАЩll pay me back if you win. If notвБ†вАФworse luck! We shall be quits. Go back to PedroвАЩs this evening, as if nothing had happened. The car will take you there; then itвАЩll come back here to take me to Lady GriffithвАЩs, where IвАЩll ask you to join me later on. I count upon it, eh? The car will fetch you from PedroвАЩs.вАЭ

He opens a drawer and takes out five notes which he hands to Vincent.

вАЬBe off with you, now.вАЭ

вАЬBut your father?вАЭ

вАЬOh, yes; I forgot to tell you: he died about.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ He pulls out his watch and exclaims: вАЬBy Jove! how late it is! Nearly midnight.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ You must make haste. Yes, about four hours ago.вАЭ

All this is said without any quickening of his voice, on the contrary with a kind of nonchalance.

вАЬAnd arenвАЩt you going to stay to.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

вАЬTo watch by the body?вАЭ interrupts Robert. вАЬNo, thatвАЩs my young brotherвАЩs business. He is up there with his old nurse, who was on better terms with the deceased than I was.вАЭ

Then as Vincent remains motionless, he goes on:

вАЬLook here, my dear fellow, I donвАЩt want to appear cynical, but I have a horror of reach-me-down sentiments. In my early days I cut out my filial love according to the pattern I had in my heart; but I soon saw that my measurements had been too ample, and I was obliged to take it in. The old man never in his life occasioned me anything but trouble and vexation and constraint. If he had any tenderness left, it was certainly not to me that he showed it. My first impulses of affection towards him, in the days before I knew how to behave, brought me nothing but snubsвБ†вАФand I learnt my lesson. You must have seen for yourself when you were attending him.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Did he ever thank you? Did you ever get the slightest look, the smallest smile from him? He always thought everything his due. Oh, he was what people call a character! I think he must have made my mother very unhappy, and yet he loved herвБ†вАФthat is, if he ever really loved anyone. I think he made everyone who came near him sufferвБ†вАФhis servants, his dogs, his horses, his mistresses; not his friends, for he had none. A general sigh of relief will go up at his death. He was, I believe, a man of great distinction in вАШhis line,вАЩ as people say; but I have never been able to discover what it was. He was very intelligent, undoubtedly. At heart, I hadвБ†вАФI still haveвБ†вАФa certain admiration for himвБ†вАФbut as for making play with a handkerchiefвБ†вАФas for wringing tears outвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ no, thank you, IвАЩm no longer child enough for that. Be off with you now! And join me in an hourвАЩs time at LilianвАЩs. What! youвАЩre not dressed? Absurd! What does it matter? But if itвАЩll make you more comfortable, IвАЩll promise not to change either. Agreed! Light a cigar before you go and send the car back quicklyвБ†вАФitвАЩll fetch you again afterwards.вАЭ

He watched Vincent go out, shrugged his shoulders, then went into his dressing-room to change into his dress suit, which was ready laid out for him on a sofa.

In a room on the first floor, the old count is lying on his deathbed. Someone has placed a crucifix on his breast, but has omitted to fold his hands over it. A beard of some daysвАЩ growth softens the stubborn angle of his chin. Beneath his grey hair, which is brushed up en brosse, the wrinkles that line his forehead seem less deeply graven, as though they were relaxed. His eye is sunk beneath the arch of the brow and the shaggy growth of the eyebrow. I know that we shall never see him again, and that is the reason that I take a long look at him. Beside the head of the bed is an armchair, in which is seated the old nurse S√©raphine. But she has risen. She goes up to a table where an old-fashioned lamp is dimly lighting the room; it needs turning up. A lampshade casts the light on to the book young Gontran is reading.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶

вАЬYouвАЩre tired, Master Gontran. You had better go to bed.вАЭ

The glance that Gontran raises from his book to rest upon S√©raphine is very gentle. His fair hair, a lock of which he pushes back from his forehead, waves loosely over his temples. He is fifteen years old, and his face, which is still almost girlish, expresses nothing as yet but tenderness and love.

вАЬAnd you?вАЭ he says. вАЬIt is you who ought to go to bed, you poor old Fine. Last night, you were on your feet nearly the whole time.вАЭ

вАЬOh, IвАЩm accustomed to sitting up. And besides I slept during the daytimeвБ†вАФbut you.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

вАЬNo, IвАЩm all right. I donвАЩt feel tired; and it does me good to stay here thinking and reading. I knew Papa so little; I think I should forget him altogether if I didnвАЩt take a good look at him now. I will sit beside him till daylight. How long is it, Fine, since you came to us?вАЭ

вАЬI came the year before you were born, and youвАЩre nearly sixteen.вАЭ

вАЬDo you remember Mamma quite well?вАЭ

вАЬDo I remember your Mamma? What a question! You might as well ask me if I remember my own name. To be sure, I remember your Mamma.вАЭ

вАЬI remember her tooвБ†вАФa little.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ But not very well.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ I was only five when she died. Used Papa to talk to her much?вАЭ

вАЬIt depended on his mood. Your Papa was never a one to talk much, and he didnвАЩt care to be spoken to first. All the same in those days he was a little more talkative than he has been of late.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ But there now! WhatвАЩs past is past, and itвАЩs better not to stir it up again. ThereвАЩs One above whoвАЩs a better judge of these things than we are.вАЭ

вАЬDo you really think that He concerns Himself about such things, dear Fine?вАЭ

вАЬWhy, if He doesnвАЩt, who should then?вАЭ

Gontran puts his lips on S√©raphineвАЩs red, roughened hand. вАЬYou really ought to go to bed now. I promise to wake you as soon as it is light, and then IвАЩll take my turn to rest. Please!вАЭ

As soon as S√©raphine has left him, Gontran falls upon his knees at the foot of the bed; he buries his head in the sheets, but he cannot succeed in weeping. No emotion stirs his heart; his eyes remain despairingly dry. Then he gets up and looks at the impassive face on the bed. At this solemn moment, he would like to have some rare, sublime experienceвБ†вАФhear a message from the world beyondвБ†вАФsend his thought flying into ethereal regions, inaccessible to mortal senses. But no! his thought remains obstinately grovelling on the earth; he looks at the dead manвАЩs bloodless hands and wonders for how much longer the nails will go on growing. The sight of the unclasped hands grates on him. He would like to join them, to make them hold the crucifix. What a good idea! He thinks of S√©raphineвАЩs astonishment when she sees the dead hands folded together; the thought of S√©raphineвАЩs astonishment amuses him; and then he despises himself for being amused. Nevertheless he stoops over the bed. He seizes the arm which is farthest from him. The arm is stiff and will not bend. Gontran tries to force it, but the whole body moves with it. He seizes the other arm, which seems a little less rigid. Gontran almost succeeds in putting the hand in the proper place. He takes the crucifix and tries to slip it between the fingers and the thumb, but the contact of the cold flesh turns him sick. He thinks he is going to faint. He has a mind to call S√©raphine back. He gives up everythingвБ†вАФthe crucifix, which drops aslant on the tumbled sheet, and the lifeless arm, which falls back again into its first position; then, through the depths of the funereal silence, he suddenly hears a rough and brutal вАЬGod damn!вАЭ which fills him with terror, as if someone else.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ He turns roundвБ†вАФbut no! he is alone. It was from his own lips, from his own heart, that that resounding curse broke forthвБ†вАФhis, who until today has never uttered an oath! Then he sits down and plunges again into his reading.
